title: Customer Concentration: The Exit Discount Most Business Owners Don't See Coming
linkedin:
Your best client just renewed. Revenue is strong heading into the new financial year.

Most owners see that as a win. A buyer sees it differently.

When one customer accounts for 30 per cent of your revenue, that is not just a strong relationship. It is the first thing a buyer will map in due diligence, and the first risk they will price into the offer.

Customer concentration is one of the most consistently underpriced factors in Australian business sales. The discount does not show up as a named line item. It shows up as a lower earnings multiple applied to the whole business, an earnout clause tied to whether the anchor client stays post-settlement, or a condition requiring written confirmation from that client before the deal can close.

The issue is not the relationship itself. It is that a buyer prices the business on what the earnings look like if that client reduces spend or renegotiates after you have left. They are not paying for the current result. They are paying for the transferable result.

The most effective fix is time. Twelve to twenty-four months of growing a second and third client tier changes how a buyer reads the same revenue structure entirely, not by shrinking the anchor relationship but by building alongside it.

With EOFY five days away, it is worth running a quick look at your own revenue split before the new year opens. The number you find might be the most important one in your exit preparation.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

The discount does not usually show up as a lower headline price. It shows up in deal structure: a reduced multiple, an earnout tied to whether the anchor client stays on, or a written condition before the deal closes.

google:
One thing many business owners discover late in an exit process is that their biggest client relationship, which looks like a strength from the inside, reads as concentration risk to a buyer.

When one customer accounts for more than 20 per cent of your revenue, buyers typically adjust how they structure the offer. The adjustment does not always appear as a lower price. It often comes through as an earnout clause, a condition requiring the anchor client to confirm in writing they will stay on after settlement, or a reduced earnings multiple applied across the whole business.

Customer concentration is one of eight areas ProfitPulse examines when working with business owners across Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria on exit readiness. The most practical way to reduce this risk is to grow the second and third tier of your client base over the 12 to 24 months before a sale, without needing to reduce the anchor relationship.

With the new financial year beginning in five days, now is a good moment to understand your own position before those months start. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
